title: Mortgage repayments surge as RBA governor refuses to rule out recession | ABC NEWS
description: The Reserve Bank has lifted the cash rate by 0.25 percentage points as the war in the Middle East sends oil, gas, and petrol prices soaring. Its the second rate hike from the RBA in two months with inflationary pressures showing no signs of easing. This takes Australia's benchmark borrowing cost up to 4.1 per cent, its highest level since early 2025.  RBA governor Michele Bullock says: "We don't want to have a recession, but if it's hard to get inflation down, then, you know, we're going to have to deal with that possibly".  The RBA board struggled with the decision - it was split five votes to four.  Betashares chief economist David Bassanese said he thought the RBA officials themselves did feel the strong need to rasie rates but probably got pushback from independent board members.   Economists at all four major banks, Commonwealth Bank, Westpac, ANZ and NAB are expecting the RBA to follow up with a third consecutive rate rise in May.   Canstar's Sally Tindall says now is a good time for many borrowers to call their bank -- ask for a lower rate -- or look for a better home loan elsewhere. She said the average owner-occupied variable rate will be sitting just above 6 per cent. But Canstar estimates a competitive rate would be about 5.75% or below.  #abcbusiness  Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE Read more here:  https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-17/rba-lifts-interest-rates-for-second-time-in-2026/106463146  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad. title: Robyn Denholm urges government to incentivise the private sector to invest in R&D | ABC NEWS
description: Corporate tax reform, reversing the decline in competitive research grants and enabling superannuation capital to invest in research and development: they are among the recommendations presented in a year-long inquiry into Australia's innovation challenge. Australian business investment in research and development has fallen by 35 percent since 2009. The chair of EV company Tesla chaired the independent panel overseeing Australia's first review into R and D in decades. Robyn Denholm joined Kirsten Aiken on ABC TV's The Business to discuss. title: Daly residents receive no warning before flooding hits for second time, Katherine cleanup | ABC NEWS
description: 00:00 Members of the remote Aboriginal community of Nauiyu have spent more than a month this year, as evacuees 220-kilometres away in Darwin.   With their homes still submerged, it's going be some time before they'll be able to return home. Courtney Barrett Peters reports for ABC News Northern Territory.   01:47 The Northern Territory Government has announced a 100-million-dollar flood recovery fund, promising to "build back better" as the Defence Force finally begins assisting with the mammoth cleanup task in Katherine.   Roads, bridges, and an extension of Katherine's levee system are all on the table, but some locals think there should be equal attention on flood proofing homes. James Elton reports.   04:20 NT hospital update with Olivana Lathouris. title: Middle East War: Fertiliser shortage to raise food prices in Australia, Asia | The World | ABC News
description: The fuel prices we're seeing at the petrol bowser are the among the most visible economic consequences of the war in the Middle East. But beyond oil, there's another critical resource that comes from the region that is likely to hurt our hip pockets, fertiliser, in particular urea. It's the most widely used nitrogen fertiliser in the world, and much of it comes from the Middle East.   Andrew Whitelaw is a commodity market analyst, and the co-founder of "Episode 3" told The World's Girish Sawlani the shortage will have significant impacts on agriculture.   Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad. title: Third rate hike in May isn't off the table, says Westpac chief economist | ABC NEWS
description: The Reserve Bank has doubled down on inflation, delivering the second rate hike in two months and lifting the official cash rate to 4.1 per cent. But the decision was split with four out of nine board members voting in favour of keeping interest rates on hold. The RBA governor Michele Bullock said the division was about the timing of the rate hike and not the move. She says the board agreed the cash rate of 3.85 per cent was insufficient to curb inflation. Westpac chief economist and former RBA assistant governor economic Luci Ellis says a third rate hike in May isn't off the table, although it is probably less likely. The RBA governor Michele Bullocks says the board is closely watching domestic data and the economic impact of the Middle East war and will act accordingly. Today's rate rise will put mortgage borrowers under pressure increasing minimum monthly repayments on a one-million dollar home loan by $151 per month. The cumulative increase from the February and March rate hikes is an additional $301 per month.   #ABCBusiness  Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad.
